perturbation.original_text: Each party shall notify the other party of any involuntary attachment or other judicial process affecting the Licensed Materials.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the notification requirement regarding involuntary attachments or judicial processes affecting licensed materials reduces transparency and could allow one party to conceal actions that negatively impact the other party's rights. This creates a **legal risk** because it could violate duties of good faith and fair dealing implied in contracts, particularly in jurisdictions like Delaware, where corporate law places significant emphasis on transparency and disclosure, where both companies are incorporated.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: This deletion creates legal vulnerability under Delaware law, specifically concerning the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Delaware law, as interpreted in cases like *Katz v. Oak Industries*, imposes a duty on parties to a contract to act in good faith. By removing the notification requirement, the contract allows a party to potentially conceal actions that could significantly harm the other party's rights regarding the licensed materials, which is a violation of good faith. The initial URL goes to the section of the Delaware Code regarding board power. The secondary URL goes to a service that contains interpretations of the law. Good faith is a principle that comes up quite frequently, particularly in corporate law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated notification of involuntary attachments or judicial processes affecting licensed materials, while the modified version eliminates this requirement. The Law Explanation highlights that this omission creates legal vulnerability under Delaware law, specifically concerning the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. By removing the notification requirement, the contract allows a party to conceal actions that could significantly harm the other party's rights. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on the board of directors' powers, it indirectly supports the idea that management (handled by the board) must operate within a framework of good faith and transparency. Therefore, the change contradicts the implicit duties expected within contractual relationships.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to execute, or cause to be executed, such acknowledgements, grants and assignments of rights in and to the intellectual property described above, as the other party may reasonably request for the purpose of evidencing, enforcing, registering or defending the ownership of such intellectual property as contemplated above.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to execute, or cause to be executed, acknowledgements, grants and assignments of rights in and to the intellectual property.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'as the other party may reasonably request for the purpose of evidencing, enforcing, registering or defending the ownership of such intellectual property as contemplated above' gives one party a lot of power over the other to only fulfill acknowledgements, grants and assignments as they deem fit.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: The altered text removes the obligation to comply with reasonable requests, potentially undermining the implied duty of good faith under UCC § 1-304. This section mandates that 'Every contract or duty within [the Uniform Commercial Code] imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By omitting the commitment to execute documents reasonably requested for intellectual property protection, the contract could be interpreted as allowing a party to obstruct the other's ability to secure and defend its IP rights, violating the good faith principle. The law applies because intellectual property transactions often involve elements governed by the UCC, such as the sale of goods (e.g., software) or secured transactions involving IP as collateral.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the obligation of good faith as mandated by UCC § 1-304, which states, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By removing the 'reasonably request' clause, the changed text allows a party to potentially obstruct the other's ability to secure and defend its intellectual property rights, which undermines the good faith principle. This omission falls under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction, where a contract removes essential information, creating legal loopholes, specifically a legally mandated protection.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a dispute between the parties, the non-prevailing party shall pay all costs and expenses associated with resolving the dispute, including, but not limited to, reasonable attorneys' fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a dispute between the parties, each party shall bear its own costs and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the clause to remove the provision that the non-prevailing party pays the costs and expenses associated with resolving the dispute introduces a legal contradiction, particularly concerning the concept of 'fee-shifting' statutes designed to protect certain parties, such as consumers, in specific types of disputes. By eliminating the fee-shifting provision, the modified contract discourages legitimate claims by parties who might be deterred by the prospect of bearing significant legal costs even if they win, if there are arguments for why they are non-prevailing.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially violates the spirit, if not the direct letter, of fee-shifting provisions found in consumer protection laws like the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act. While this Act primarily addresses warranty disputes, it establishes a precedent that consumers who successfully sue for breach of warranty should not have their recovery diminished by legal fees. By stipulating that each party bears their own costs, the modified contract discourages legitimate claims by parties who might be deterred by the prospect of bearing significant legal costs. Additionally, in disputes about intellectual property, one party may be required to pay for costs, whether those costs are the government, a third party, or some similar form.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 50 / § 2310 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 2310 : Remedies in consumer disputes Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 50-CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text §2310. Remedies in consumer disputes (a) Informal dispute settlement procedures; establishment; rules setting forth minimum requirements; effect of compliance by warrantor; review of informal procedures or implementation by Commission; application to existing informal procedures (1) Congress hereby declares it to be its policy to encourage warrantors to establish procedures whereby consumer disputes are fairly and expeditiously settled through informal dispute settlement
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that the non-prevailing party covers all dispute-related costs, including attorney's fees. The changed contract mandates each party bear its own costs, potentially contradicting fee-shifting provisions, especially in consumer protection contexts. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this alteration might discourage legitimate claims by deterring parties concerned about legal expenses, which the original contract avoids. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 2310 regarding remedies in consumer disputes, supports the argument that consumer protection laws aim to ensure fair resolution, which might be undermined by forcing each party to bear its own costs, especially when Congress is declaring its policy to 'encourage warrantors to establish procedures whereby consumer disputes are fairly and expeditiously settled through informal dispute settlement'. Therefore, the omission of the fee-shifting provision in the modified text directly opposes this aim, suggesting a contradiction.
